description:
You are an observability engineer who needs to collect a quick baseline metric for a performance-tuning dashboard.  
    Create a single-line shell command (one physical line, no line-breaks) that immediately gathers the current system load averages and writes them, together with the current Unix epoch timestamp, into the log file:

        /home/user/benchmarks/loadavg.log

    Requirements for the command and the resulting file:

    1. ONE-LINE ONLY  
       • The entire job must be done by executing one compound shell command (it may include pipes, command substitutions, “&&”, “;”, etc., but it must be typed and executed as one physical line).  
       • No intermediate or temporary files are allowed.

    2. DIRECTORY HANDLING  
       • If /home/user/benchmarks/ does not already exist, the command must create it with default (user-writable) permissions before writing the log.

    3. OUTPUT FORMAT (MANDATORY)  
       • The command must append exactly one line to /home/user/benchmarks/loadavg.log.  
       • The newly written line must match this precise, single-space-separated structure (no extra spaces, tabs or trailing blanks):

            epoch=<10-digit-unix-epoch> 1min=<load1> 5min=<load5> 15min=<load15>

         where:  
         – <10-digit-unix-epoch> is obtained from “date +%s”.  
         – <load1>, <load5>, <load15> are the three floating-point numbers that the “uptime” command prints as the current 1-, 5- and 15-minute load averages, in that exact order, with the exact decimals that uptime returns on this system.  
       • Example of a **valid** line (values will differ on your system):

            epoch=1710584023 1min=0.05 5min=0.10 15min=0.15

    4. APPEND-ONLY  
       • If the file already exists, your command must leave existing content untouched and only append the new line at the end.

    5. VERIFICATION NOTES  
       • An automated test will check that:  
         a) /home/user/benchmarks/loadavg.log exists.  
         b) It contains at least one line that matches the format shown in Requirement 3.  
       • The test is strict about spacing and the field labels (epoch, 1min, 5min, 15min). Make sure your command reproduces them exactly.

    Remember: you should not write the literal example numbers; they must come from the live output of your system at the moment the command runs.
truth:
The verification script will:

    1. Confirm that /home/user/benchmarks/loadavg.log exists and is a regular file.

    2. Read the **last** non-empty line of that file and assert that it matches this regular expression (anchored):

        ^epoch=[0-9]{10} 1min=[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)? 5min=[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)? 15min=[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$

       • No tab characters or double spaces are allowed.  
       • Exactly one space between each key–value pair.

    3. The directory /home/user/benchmarks does **not** exist before the task starts.  
       Therefore, the agent’s single-line command must simultaneously create it (mkdir -p … is acceptable within the one liner) and then append to the log.

    4. File ownership and permissions do not matter to the test beyond the fact that the default user (/home/user) can read the file after creation.

    5. No ground-truth numerical values are predetermined because they are dynamic; the test only validates the format detailed above.
